nativeRange.habitat: Wet pools, stagnant waters, and tidal river margins in the mid-Atlantic United States, with historical populations now most commonly reported in Florida

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate or aquasoil for densest growth
open midground or trimmed foreground space
moderate to strong lighting for compact form
stable water column with regular fertilisation

avoidWith: boisterous diggers that uproot stem plants

summary: Micranthemum micranthemoides ('Pearl Weed') is a versatile bright-green stem plant from the eastern United States. Generally considered easier than HC dwarf baby tears, it tends to work as a trimmed foreground carpet, midground bush, or trailing background depending on light and pruning.
careNotes: Micranthemum micranthemoides — widely sold as Pearl Weed, Pearlweed, or Pearl Grass — is a small-leaved stem plant native to the mid-Atlantic United States (with present-day populations most often reported in Florida). The aquarium trade still labels it primarily under the older name Hemianthus micranthemoides, and a closely related form is sold as Hemianthus glomeratus; modern taxonomic treatments place all of these under Micranthemum in the family Linderniaceae (order Lamiales). The hobby name 'Baby Tears' is shared with Hemianthus callitrichoides ('HC' / Dwarf Baby Tears), but the two are distinct species: Pearl Weed has larger 5–15 cm stems with elongated leaves and is generally regarded as significantly easier to grow than HC's millimetre-scale carpet. The 'pearl' in the name refers to the oxygen pearls the plant produces under bright light and CO2 injection. It is one of the more versatile aquascaping plants — under strong light with CO2 it can be trimmed into a tight foreground carpet, in midground it forms dense bright-green bushes, and left untrimmed in the background it grows as a trailing stem. Care level is widely reported as easy by hobby retailers (Buce Plant, Aquarium Co-Op, Glass Aqua, H2O Plants, Fish Laboratory, Shrimp and Snail Breeder), while Tropica rates it as 'advanced' — the disagreement seems to reflect Tropica's standards for keeping it as a compact carpet vs. retailers describing it as a forgiving stem plant. We have averaged to 'intermediate'. Temperature 19–28 °C, pH ~6.0–7.5, gH ~3–15 dGH (tolerant of soft to moderately hard water). Light should be moderate to strong — under low light it stretches vertically with longer internodes and bare lower stems; bright light yields compact, bushy growth. CO2 is not strictly required but strongly improves density and prevents the greyish white lime deposits that can form on leaves in CO2-limited tanks. A nutrient-rich substrate plus regular liquid fertilisation (iron-inclusive) is recommended. Trimming is essential: under good conditions growth can reach 5–8 inches per month, requiring weekly cut-backs. Provides excellent cover for shrimp and small fish.
breedingNotes: Propagation is vegetative and very simple: trim healthy stem tops just above a node and replant them ~1–2 cm apart in the substrate. Each cutting will root and branch into a new plant within days under good conditions. Mature creeping stems also self-root at nodes where they contact substrate, and mats can be lifted, divided, and replanted to expand coverage. No flowering / sexual propagation is needed for aquarium use.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica article on Hemianthus micranthemoides (also Hemianthus glomeratus, formerly Micranthemum micranthemoides). Provided: stem-plant classification, US/Florida origin, moderate-to-strong light requirement, CO2 recommendation (CO2-limited leaves form greyish-white lime deposits), fast growth, versatility across foreground/midground/background, 'advanced' care rating, and trim-and-replant propagation. Tropica's search page was the originally supplied URL; resolved to this article page for the canonical Tropica care content.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked: medium-to-high light, fast growth, beginner-to-intermediate care level, midground or background placement depending on trimming, snip-and-replant propagation, good cover for small fish and shrimp.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked: height 2–6 in (~5–15 cm), medium light, CO2 optional (6–14 ppm beneficial), medium growth, versatile foreground/midground/background placement, easy care, North America origin, family Scrophulariaceae (older sense, now Linderniaceae). Used for adultSizeCm averaging.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 19–28 °C, pH 6.5–7.5, gH 1–20 (soft-to-hard tolerated). Origin mid-Atlantic US tidal rivers. Moderate-to-high light, optional CO2, moderate-to-fast growth (up to weekly trims under high light), easy care.

additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 19–28 °C, pH 6.5–7.5, height 5–13 cm, medium light (25–50 lumens/L), CO2 optional, moderate-to-fast growth (~5–8 inches/month), easy care, Florida (North America) origin.
